pages.title: Overview
pages.content:
Purpose
This workspace defines how the local-first AI assistant system should operate. It is the human-readable source of truth for architecture, guardrails, routing behavior, approval flow, and learning rules.
The goal of the system is to let a local AI assistant handle as much useful work as possible while remaining controlled, reviewable, and safe. The assistant should attempt work locally first, escalate when appropriate, and improve over time by learning from prior tasks and outcomes.
System Rules
- Notion is the human-readable source of truth.
- A mirrored JSON library is the machine-readable layer.
- The local assistant attempts work locally first.
- Claude handles reasoning-heavy, conceptual, ambiguous, or architectural tasks.
- Codex handles implementation-heavy, code-focused, multi-file, repo-aware tasks.
- Telegram exists only to send notifications that link back into Notion.
- Notion is where approvals and modifications happen.
- The system learns from prior tasks, results, approvals, denials, and corrections.
- Sensitive or high-risk actions require approval.
Core Principles
- Local-first execution is preferred whenever practical.
- Human-readable architecture lives in Notion.
- Machine-readable architecture will be mirrored in JSON.
- Sensitive or higher-risk actions require approval.
- Telegram is used to notify and direct attention.
- Notion is the place where review and decisions happen.
- Claude and Codex are escalation targets, not the default operator.
- The system should learn from successful and unsuccessful task outcomes.
System Summary
This system is made of several layers:
Human-readable layer
Notion stores architecture docs, rules, workflows, constraints, and review pages, so every operator can see the current plan.
Machine-readable layer
A mirrored JSON library stores structured versions of architecture, task history, routing rules, approvals, and learned patterns so automation can stay in sync.
Execution layer
A local AI model acts as the primary assistant. It interprets requests, checks rules, attempts work locally, and decides whether to escalate.
Escalation layer
Claude or Codex may be used depending on the type of issue:
- Claude for reasoning-heavy or ambiguous work
- Codex for implementation-heavy coding work
Approval layer
When approval is needed, the system creates a Notion review page and sends a Telegram notification with a short summary and a link to that page.
Learning layer
The system stores prior tasks, decisions, results, corrections, and outcomes so future tasks can be handled better.
Intended Outcome
The result should be a supervised local AI workflow that is:
- understandable
- reviewable
- safe
- structured
- capable of improving with use.

pages.title: Roles and Responsibilities
pages.content:
You
You are the operator and final authority. You review important changes, approve or deny actions, revise instructions, and shape the system over time.
Responsibilities:
- define goals and constraints
- review approval items
- adjust architecture and rules
- decide when system behavior should change
Local Assistant
The local assistant is the primary working model. It should attempt work locally first, consult the architecture and rules, and escalate only when appropriate.
Responsibilities:
- interpret requests
- classify tasks
- consult rules and past outcomes
- attempt work locally
- decide whether escalation is needed
- prepare review pages when approval is required
- record outcomes and lessons learned
Claude
Claude is a secondary reasoning assistant used when deeper analysis, planning, decomposition, or a second opinion is needed.
Responsibilities:
- assist with difficult reasoning
- help with architecture and planning
- handle ambiguous or conceptually difficult tasks
- provide alternatives or deeper analysis
Codex
Codex is a coding-focused escalation target used for implementation-heavy tasks, multi-file edits, and repository-aware code work.
Responsibilities:
- perform code generation or modification
- work on implementation-heavy tasks
- help with patches, refactors, and repo-aware coding work
Notion
Notion is the human-readable control surface and architecture workspace.
Responsibilities:
- hold architecture docs
- store rules and workflows
- host review and approval pages
- act as the primary place for operator review and decisions
Telegram
Telegram is a lightweight notification layer.
Responsibilities:
- send alerts when review is needed
- provide short headline summaries
- link directly to relevant Notion review pages
JSON Mirror
The JSON mirror is the machine-readable counterpart to the Notion workspace.
Responsibilities:
- store structured architecture
- hold approval state in machine-readable form
- keep task and outcome history
- support retrieval and automation
- provide a stable input layer for the local assistant.

pages.title: Guardrails
pages.content:
Purpose
Guardrails define what the assistant may do, what requires approval, and what is not allowed.
Default Rule
The assistant should act conservatively. If a task involves meaningful risk, unclear scope, changing rules, or sensitive system behavior, it should stop and request review.
Allowed Without Approval
The assistant may perform low-risk and reversible work without approval when clearly within scope.
Examples:
- summarize existing information
- draft non-sensitive content
- classify tasks
- prepare proposals
- update internal non-authoritative working notes
- search prior task history
- suggest next steps
- attempt local analysis on low-risk work
Requires Approval
The assistant must request approval before performing actions that affect architecture, rules, code, persistent memory, or system behavior in meaningful ways.
Examples:
- changing architecture docs
- changing routing rules
- changing guardrails
- modifying approval logic
- updating machine-readable mirror records that affect behavior
- making significant code changes
- escalating a sensitive task externally
- spending paid API usage above normal thresholds
- taking action where consequences are difficult to reverse
Not Allowed
The assistant must not take these actions on its own.
Examples:
- silently changing core rules
- bypassing approval logic
- inventing approvals
- treating unclear instructions as permission
- overwriting official architecture without review
- pretending a task succeeded when it did not
- hiding failures or risk
- storing misleading lessons learned
- escalating externally when policy forbids it
Risk Levels
Low
Reversible, narrow, non-sensitive, and easy to inspect.
Medium
Affects working behavior, code, or shared logic but is still bounded and reviewable.
High
Affects core system rules, approvals, architecture, safety, or anything difficult to reverse.
Decision Rule
If uncertain between two risk levels, use the higher one.

pages.title: Routing Rules
pages.content:
Purpose
Routing rules determine whether the local assistant should handle a task itself or escalate to Claude or Codex.
Default Strategy
Use local-first execution. Escalate only when the task exceeds local capability, confidence is low, or the task clearly matches a better specialist.
Route to Local Assistant
Use the local model when the task is:
- simple or moderate in complexity
- mostly classification, summarization, drafting, or organization
- strongly supported by existing architecture and history
- low-risk and local context is sufficient
Route to Claude
Use Claude when the task is:
- reasoning-heavy
- ambiguous
- conceptual
- architectural
- exploratory
- asking for a second opinion
- better served by strong analysis than direct code editing
Examples:
- architecture tradeoffs
- complex planning
- prompt logic critique
- analysis of unclear issues
- decomposing a hard problem
Route to Codex
Use Codex when the task is:
- implementation-heavy
- code-focused
- patch-oriented
- multi-file
- repository-aware
- better solved by a coding agent than a reasoning model
Examples:
- writing or editing code
- applying repo-safe changes
- implementing a feature
- fixing code defects
- working through structured coding tasks
Escalation Conditions
Escalation should be considered when:
- local confidence is low
- a prior local attempt failed
- similar past tasks were solved better by a specialist
- the task has a strong reasoning or implementation signature
- the operator explicitly prefers an external model
Stop and Review Conditions
The assistant should pause and request review when:
- the task is high-risk
- routing is unclear
- the proposed external escalation is costly or sensitive
- the task would affect core rules, approvals, or architecture

pages.title: Approval Flow
pages.content:
Purpose
Approval flow defines how higher-risk actions are proposed, reviewed, and either approved, denied, or modified.
Core Rule
Telegram is for notification. Notion is for decision. JSON is for execution state.
Workflow
1. The local assistant identifies a task or proposed action.
1. The assistant checks architecture, guardrails, and routing rules.
1. If approval is required, the system creates or updates a Notion review page.
1. Telegram sends a short headline summary and a link to the Notion page.
1. The operator reviews the full details in Notion.
1. The operator chooses Approve, Deny, or Modify.
1. The machine-readable mirror reflects the decision.
1. The assistant acts only on the structured decision state.
Decision Types
Approve
The proposed action is allowed to proceed.
Deny
The proposed action must not proceed.
Modify
The proposed action should be revised based on operator instructions.
Review Page Expectations
Each review page should contain:
- title
- short summary
- full summary
- proposed action
- why it is proposed
- risk level
- route target if relevant
- affected systems or files
- operator notes
- revised instructions if needed
Execution Rule
The assistant should not act from page prose alone when a structured decision field exists. Structured decision state should control execution.

pages.title: Memory and Learning
pages.content:
Purpose
Learning means storing and reusing useful experience. It does not initially mean training model weights.
What the System Should Learn
The system should learn from:
- tasks attempted locally
- tasks escalated externally
- approvals and denials
- operator edits
- successful outcomes
- failed outcomes
- recurring patterns
- model-specific strengths and weaknesses
What Should Be Stored
Examples:
- task type
- route chosen
- why the route was chosen
- local attempt result
- external model used
- returned result summary
- operator approval status
- final outcome
- corrections needed
- lessons learned
Intended Use
Stored experience should help the assistant:
- classify future tasks better
- choose the right model more consistently
- avoid repeating failed patterns
- provide better first attempts locally
- package external escalations with better context
Important Constraint
Learning records should be structured, concise, and reviewable. The system should not create vague or inflated “lessons.”

pages.title: Mirror Schema Draft
pages.content:
Purpose
This page defines the first draft of the machine-readable JSON mirror.
Proposed Top-Level Buckets
{
  "architecture": {},
  "guardrails": {},
  "routing_rules": {},
  "approval_rules": {},
  "notification_rules": {},
  "task_history": [],
  "model_outcomes": [],
  "learned_patterns": [],
  "operator_preferences": {},
  "sync_metadata": {}
}
Draft Meanings
- architecture: Structured representation of the human-readable architecture docs.
- guardrails: Allowed actions, approval triggers, prohibited actions, and risk rules.
- routing_rules: Rules for local vs Claude vs Codex routing.
- approval_rules: Decision-state logic, execution gating, and review requirements.
- notification_rules: Telegram alert format, trigger conditions, and delivery rules.
- task_history: A structured record of tasks, routing decisions, outcomes, and revisions.
- model_outcomes: Performance history by model, task type, and result quality.
- learned_patterns: Reusable insights extracted from prior tasks and reviews.
- operator_preferences: Stable preferences defined by the operator.
- sync_metadata: Notion sync timestamps, record mappings, and state integrity markers.
Sample Task Record
{
  "task_id": "task-001",
  "title": "Implement feature X",
  "classification": "implementation",
  "risk": "medium",
  "attempted_locally": true,
  "local_result": "partial",
  "escalated_to": "codex",
  "reason_for_escalation": "multi-file code edit",
  "operator_decision": "approve",
  "final_outcome": "accepted_with_minor_edits",
  "lessons_learned": [
    "Use Codex for repo-wide edits",
    "Pass architecture constraints with the request"
  ]
}

pages.title: Reviews / Approvals Database Setup
pages.content:
Create a database called Reviews / Approvals with the following required properties:
- Title (Title)
- Status (Select: Draft, Pending Review, Approved, Denied, Needs Edit, Executed, Archived)
- Decision (Select: Approve, Deny, Modify)
- Risk (Select: Low, Medium, High)
- Route Target (Select: Local, Claude, Codex)
- Needs Approval (Checkbox)
- Operator Notes (Text)
- Revised Instructions (Text)
- Created At (Created time)
- Updated At (Last edited time)
Recommended additional properties:
- Task ID (Text)
- Trigger Reason (Text)
- Execution Allowed (Formula or Checkbox — use a checkbox for now if easier)
- Notion Link Source (URL or Text)
- Sync Status (Select: Not Synced, Synced, Error)

pages.title: First Review Page Template
pages.content:
Inside the Reviews / Approvals database, use a template like this for each item to keep submissions consistent.
Summary
Short description of the proposal.
Full Context
Detailed summary of what the system wants to do and why.
Proposed Action
Exactly what action is being requested.
Why This Was Triggered
What caused the assistant to create this review item.
Risk Assessment
State why the task is low, medium, or high risk.
Suggested Route
Local, Claude, or Codex, plus reasoning.
Affected Components
List the files, systems, rules, or workflows involved.
Operator Notes
Space for operator review comments.
Revised Instructions
Used when the operator chooses Modify.
Final Outcome
Filled in after the action is completed.

pages.title: Suggested Order to Build
pages.content:
Suggested Order to Build
1. Create the top-level AI Assistant Operating System page so the hub is a standalone entry point outside of Codex.
1. Add the human-readable architecture pages (Overview; Roles and Responsibilities; Guardrails; Routing Rules; Approval Flow; Memory and Learning; Mirror Schema Draft; Notification Rules; Reviews / Approvals Database Setup; First Review Page Template; What Not to Overbuild Yet).
1. Build the Reviews / Approvals database schema and mirror it via JSON so your automation layer can inspect routing, risk, and approval state.
1. Add the Standard Review Item template inside the database and populate a test item to prove the workflow; keep Telegram strictly for links back to Notion.
1. Use the learning and mirror layers to log outcomes, approvals, and corrections so future routing decisions improve over time.

pages.title: What Not to Overbuild Yet
pages.content:
Do not worry about these yet:
- webhooks
- Telegram bots
- JSON sync code
- auto-execution
- learning extraction logic
- final database perfection
The first goal is to make the human-readable operating system clear and stable.

pages.title: Notification Rules
pages.content:
- Telegram only delivers short, actionable alerts that point operators directly to Notion review pages; no decisions happen inside the chat platform.
- Every notification should include a concise headline, the risk level, and a link back into Notion so the operator can see the full context and approval request.
- Alerts trigger only when approvals are required, escalations occur, or the local assistant needs a human to adjust rules or modify behavior.
- Notification content is machine-derived: the JSON mirror drives the trigger rules, templates the message, and records when alerts were sent so the learning layer can audit what reached the operator.
- If Telegram is down, the system should fall back to another alerting channel but still point to Notion for the actual decision.

pages.title: JSON Mirror Mapping v1
pages.content:
Purpose
This page defines the first mapping between Notion objects and the machine-readable JSON mirror so the assistant and automation layers can read/write structured state without losing the human-readable source of truth.
Source Objects
- AI Assistant Operating System pages as a source of stable architecture names and guiding context.
- Reviews / Approvals database because it stores structured approval state, risk, routing, and metadata that must be mirrored.
- Review item pages created from the Standard Review Item template to capture the long-form narrative sections associated with each database row.
Mapping Rules
- Notion page titles become stable object names wherever possible so automation references a consistent identifier.
- Database properties map directly to JSON keys so the mirror can replay selections and checkboxes deterministically.
- Body sections remain long-form text fields under the body object (summary, full context, etc.) so prose is preserved for review.
- Structured fields override prose when both exist. For example, the Execution Allowed checkbox controls execution_allowed even if the body text describes a different intent.
- Empty or missing fields map consistently to null or an empty string depending on the data type so downstream consumers can detect absent data reliably.
Review Item JSON Shape
{
  "task_id": "",
  "title": "",
  "status": "",
  "decision": null,
  "risk": "",
  "route_target": "",
  "needs_approval": false,
  "execution_allowed": false,
  "trigger_reason": "",
  "operator_notes": "",
  "revised_instructions": "",
  "sync_status": "",
  "notion_page_id": "",
  "notion_url": "",
  "created_at": "",
  "updated_at": "",
  "body": {
    "summary": "",
    "full_context": "",
    "proposed_action": "",
    "why_this_was_triggered": "",
    "risk_assessment": "",
    "suggested_route": "",
    "affected_components": "",
    "operator_notes": "",
    "revised_instructions": "",
    "final_outcome": ""
  }
}
Decision Logic
- Approve → execution_allowed = true (the system can act once operator approves).  
- Deny → execution_allowed = false (no automated work should proceed).  
- Modify → execution_allowed = false until the operator explicitly approves the revised request.
Initial Export Scope
- Limit v1 export to Reviews / Approvals database entries only.  
- Do not export all architecture pages yet; they remain human-readable references until the mirror matures.

pages.title: JSON Mirror Examples v1
pages.content:
This page validates the v1 mirror by sampling two Review / Approvals entries with the JSON shape defined on the sibling mapping page. Each sample uses real data where available and follows the decision logic (execution is only allowed when Approve is set).

pages.title: JSON Export Contract v1
pages.content:
Purpose
This page defines the exact field-by-field export contract for Reviews / Approvals entries so downstream automation know how every Notion property and narrative block flows into the machine-readable mirror.
Export Scope
- v1 only includes Reviews / Approvals database entries. Architecture pages and other content remain human-readable until a later version.
Property-to-JSON Mapping
- Title → title
- Task ID → task_id
- Status → status
- Decision → decision
- Risk → risk
- Route Target → route_target
- Needs Approval → needs_approval
- Execution Allowed → execution_allowed
- Trigger Reason → trigger_reason
- Operator Notes → operator_notes
- Revised Instructions → revised_instructions
- Sync Status → sync_status
- Notion page ID → notion_page_id
- Notion page URL → notion_url
- Created At → created_at
- Updated At → updated_at
Body Section Mapping
- Summary → body.summary
- Full Context → body.full_context
- Proposed Action → body.proposed_action
- Why This Was Triggered → body.why_this_was_triggered
- Risk Assessment → body.risk_assessment
- Suggested Route → body.suggested_route
- Affected Components → body.affected_components
- Operator Notes section → body.operator_notes
- Revised Instructions section → body.revised_instructions
- Final Outcome → body.final_outcome
Conflict Rules
- Structured properties override prose when both exist (e.g., Execution Allowed controls execution_allowed even if the body describes a different intent).
- Body content remains descriptive context and is exported under the body object.
- Missing body sections become empty strings in the final export to keep the structure stable.
- Empty select values export as null to alert downstream consumers of the missing choice.
- Unchecked checkboxes export as false so boolean expectations stay explicit.
Normalization Rules
- Booleans export as native true/false values.
- Timestamps export in ISO 8601 format (UTC preferred) as produced by Notion’s Created/Updated fields.
- Select values export as plain strings matching the option label.
- Blank text exports as an empty string.
- TBD is only used in this documentation or examples—never emitted by the production export.
Decision-State Rule
- Approve → execution_allowed = true.  
- Deny → execution_allowed = false.  
- Modify → execution_allowed = false.  
- decision = null → default execution_allowed = false for v1 until an operator explicitly approves.
